title: THE HEIR — EXPERIMENT: single-voice performance (London + the flat)
notes: One voice (George) performs narrator and ALL characters, audiobook-style; character separation is done only with v3 expression tags (register cues at each speaker change). Same screenplay text as act1-opening. Purpose: hear whether expression controls alone can carry character differentiation.

NADIR.direction: the neutral register: warm, quick, hedging

OSMAN.direction: character work: frail old man, trembling

LENA.direction: character work: lighter, wry, feminine lilt

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, dry] London. Rain on tall Georgian windows, and the civilised hum of a place where the worst thing that can happen to a man is a deadline. Nadir — younger in the face, looser in the shoulders, a good suit worn like a dressing gown — stands at the window with an old man in a donated coat: Osman, seventies, a schoolteacher's stoop, two fingers missing from his right hand.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [warm, animated] It's granted, Osman. It's over. The tribunal accepted everything — you, your wife, your son's family when we can get them out. Nobody can send you back. Not ever. [gently] You're a free man in a boring country, which is the best kind there is.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] The old man takes this in. Then he takes Nadir's hand in both of his — the whole hand, the missing fingers and all — and holds it, and can't speak. Nadir lets him hold it as long as he needs.

segments.speaker: OSMAN
segments.text: [as a frail old man, voice cracked and trembling, heavy accent of grief] They told me, in the cells — nobody leaves, nobody is heard. [tearful, almost a whisper] Forty years they told me that.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [back to the warm register, softly] I know. They told my mother the same thing. [brighter] Go and tell your wife. Use my office. Take your time on the phone — it's a good phone, it's the firm's.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, dry] The colleague walks Osman out. Nadir watches them go, and for a moment, alone, he flexes his own right hand — all five fingers — like a man checking his luck.

segments.speaker: COLLEAGUE
segments.text: [in a breezier, teasing voice] That's the fourth one this year from that godawful place. Chambers grumbles, you know. The hours you sink into them. There's no money in it.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [wry] There's no money in it. [lighter] That's rather the point. It's the only file in this building where I know exactly who I'm against.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] On the desk his phone lights and buzzes — a long foreign number — and something behind his face quietly shuts. He turns the phone face down without answering it.

segments.speaker: COLLEAGUE
segments.text: [breezy, curious] You're not going to get that?

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [dry, a little too quick] It's nobody. A wrong number that's been wrong for about fifteen years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, slowing] The phone buzzes again under his hand, patient, face down, and he doesn't look at it, [pause] and he doesn't quite manage not to.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, dry] Their flat. Small and warm and real — books, a radiator that works, a life with no motorcades in it. Lena has set her reading down. Nadir stands at the window with the foreign phone finally against his ear, and he has been listening a long time. When he speaks, the ease has gone out of his voice.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [quiet, careful] How long.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He listens.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [tighter] And he asked for me. He used those words. [pause] You're sure he used those words.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] He listens, then lowers the phone and ends the call without a goodbye, because no goodbye fits.

segments.speaker: LENA
segments.text: [in a lighter, wry, feminine register, level] That was the number you don't answer.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [back to the neutral register, exhales] My father's dying. Weeks. They think less.

segments.speaker: LENA
segments.text: [lighter register, gently] Okay. [pause] Do you want to go.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [flat] No.

segments.speaker: LENA
segments.text: [lighter register, patient, precise] That isn't what I asked. I know you don't want to. [pause] Do you need to.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] He looks out at the city that took him in — the one place on earth where no one he loves has ever been put into a car and not come back.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [deciding as he says it] I'll go and put him in the ground, and I'll come home. Three days. I'm not going anywhere near the rest of it, Lena. I bury him, I come back, [firmer] and we never say the name of that country in this flat again.

segments.speaker: LENA
segments.text: [lighter register, dry] Three days. Fine. I'm coming with you.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [alarmed] You are absolutely not —

segments.speaker: LENA
segments.text: [lighter register, overriding, calm] You're going to bury the man who made you flinch at telephones, surrounded by people who all want something from you, and you'd like to do that alone. No. I've heard about that country my whole marriage and never once seen it. [softer] Three days, both of us, and I'll finally know what I'm married to.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, quiet] She crosses and stands with him at the glass. Neither of them believes the three days, but it is a kind thing to say, so they say it.
